county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fires in 1830 and 1865 destroyed many court records and deeds. Some estate records and wills survived.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Known as 'Mother of Western NC counties' — spawned multiple daughter counties including Henderson, Madison, Haywood, and others. Earliest deed index shows 1783 but refers to Tennessee lands due to boundary confusion at formation. Named for Col. Edward Buncombe.
county_metadata.research_tips: Check parent counties (Burke/Rutherford) and grandparent Rowan for pre-1791 records. Published deed index 1783-1850 available. Qualifying ancestors eligible for First Families of Old Buncombe (FFOB). Old Buncombe County Genealogical Society maintains research materials. Land grants at NC Secretary of State. DigitalNC has extensive Asheville newspaper collection (11,621 issues Asheville Citizen alone).
county_metadata.heritage_book: The Heritage of Old Buncombe County, North Carolina (Vols 1 & 2, Doris Cline Ward, Old Buncombe County Genealogical Society)

source_profiles.source_name: Buncombe County Deed Records and Published Index 1783-1850
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online deed database from late 1700s. Published index and abstracts cover 1783-1850. Birth records 1887+, marriage 1842+, death 1913+.
source_profiles.survival_notes: 1830 and 1865 fires destroyed originals; published index available.

source_profiles.source_name: Buncombe County Wills and Probate Records 1792-1964
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Abstracts of wills 1792-1904; will records 1831-1964; probate records from 1832.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some wills survived courthouse fires; incomplete pre-1831.

source_profiles.source_name: DigitalNC Buncombe County Historic Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Asheville Citizen (11,621 issues, 1885-1926), Asheville Times (5,079 issues, 1897-1917), Highland Messenger (1840, first Asheville newspaper).

source_profiles.source_name: Buncombe County Church Records 1828-1990
source_profiles.coverage_notes: 41 church record sources. Mount Pleasant Methodist, Asheville First Baptist (1829-1881), Black Mountain United Methodist (1890-1990), Mountain View Baptist (1828-1978).

county_metadata.record_losses: April 17, 1865: Stoneman's Raid (Union cavalry) intentionally destroyed most records. Lost: most deeds prior to April 1865, most court records. Survived: ~90 wills, copies of estate records, some tax and road records at NC Archives. 6,000+ land grants lost but copies recovered from NC Secretary of State files.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Piedmont/mountain county. Parent of Buncombe (1791), Caldwell (1841), and others. For deeds prior to 1777, must consult Rowan County or Salisbury records. Classic severe 'burned county' — Stoneman reportedly spared the fireproof building itself but records inside were destroyed.
county_metadata.research_tips: Check Rowan County for pre-1777. Published deed abstracts (1777-1800, Heritage Books, 2 volumes) are essential. ~90 surviving wills are key probate source. Land grants (1779-1803) recovered at NC Archives and Burke County Library NC Room. Tax lists, road records, and Morgan District Superior Court records survived at NC Archives. Burke County Genealogical Society (PO Box 661, Morganton).
county_metadata.heritage_book: The Heritage of Burke County, NC Volume II (2001, Burke County Historical Society, 575 pages)
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: indexes only; online access through Courthouse Computer Systems. Pre-1865 deeds lost.

source_profiles.source_name: Burke County Land Records and Miscellaneous Records 1777-1800 (Published Abstracts)
source_profiles.repository: Heritage Books, Inc. / Burke County Library NC Room
source_profiles.coverage_notes: 2 volumes of published abstracts. Vol. 1 covers 1778, Vol. 2 covers 1779-1790 and miscellaneous 1777-1800. Originals destroyed 1865.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Original deeds destroyed April 17, 1865 (Stoneman's Raid). Published abstracts only.

source_profiles.source_name: Burke County Wills and Probate Abstracts 1777-1910
source_profiles.coverage_notes: ~90 wills survived 1865 destruction. Probate abstracts 1777-1910. Administrators bonds 1869-1896. Record of administrators 1897-1922.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Only ~90 wills survived from pre-1865. Post-1865 complete.

source_profiles.source_name: Burke County Land Grants 1779-1803
source_profiles.repository: NC Secretary of State / Burke County Library NC Room
source_profiles.coverage_notes: 6,000+ land grants. Local copies lost but recovered from NC Secretary of State files. Now at Burke County Library NC Room and NC Archives.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Originals at state level; local copies recovered.

source_profiles.source_name: DigitalNC Burke County Historic Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: News-Herald/Burke County News (2,112 issues, 1883-1922), Blue Ridge Blade (78 issues, 1876-1881).

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fire 1874/1876 (sources vary) — limited impact, only a few court records affected. Record survival is generally excellent from 1792.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Piedmont county. Concord established 1798. Site of first authenticated American gold discovery 1799 (Reed Gold Mine, Conrad Reed). Named for Stephen Cabarrus, Speaker of NC House of Commons.
county_metadata.research_tips: Excellent record survival despite technical burned status. ROD online from 1792. Land records comprehensive. Marriage bonds from 1792. Birth/death from 1913. Reed Gold Mine visitor center has genealogy library. Cabarrus Genealogy Society has publications. 65 church record sources on FamilySearch including colonial-era St. John's/Dutch Buffalo Creek Church (est. ~1745). 230+ cemetery record sources.
county_metadata.heritage_book: The Heritage of Cabarrus County, North Carolina (1998, Walsworth Publishing)
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: images and indexes; land records comprehensive from 1792. Vital record indexes online (images restricted).

source_profiles.source_name: Cabarrus County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed book abstracts and full indexes from formation. Published abstracts Books 1-4 (1791-1803).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from formation; minimal loss from 1876 fire.

source_profiles.source_name: Cabarrus County Wills and Probate Records 1794-1966
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of Wills 1794-1966; Will Books 1-13 (1796-1966); Index to Will Books 1843-1964; Unprocessed wills 1794-1970.

source_profiles.source_name: Cabarrus County Marriage Bonds and Records 1792-present
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage bonds from 1792. Formal marriage records from 1868 forward.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete coverage; bonds are partial (optional documents).

source_profiles.source_name: DigitalNC Cabarrus County Historic Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Concord Times (3,498 issues, 1875-1927), Concord Daily Tribune (1,300+ issues, 1910-1930), The Standard/Concord (638 issues, 1888-1902).

source_profiles.source_name: Cabarrus County Church Records 1745-1970
source_profiles.coverage_notes: 65 church record sources. St. John's/Dutch Buffalo Creek Church (est. ~1745), St. John's Evangelical Lutheran, Rocky River Church.

county_metadata.record_losses: Some original wills missing from early records. No major courthouse fire.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed January 11, 1841 from Burke and Wilkes counties to serve mountainous region where travel to parent courthouses was burdensome. Named for Joseph Caldwell, first president of UNC. Mountain county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Check Burke and Wilkes county records for pre-1841 ancestry. Online deed indexes from 1840; images from early 1900s forward. Caldwell County Genealogical Society (PO Box 2476, Lenoir) maintains local collections. Heritage book (1983) covers community histories.
county_metadata.heritage_book: The Heritage of Caldwell County, North Carolina (Vol 1, E. Carl Anderson Jr. & John O. Hawkins, 1983, Hunter Publishing)
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: images and indexes; indexes cover entire county history; images from early 1900s forward

source_profiles.source_name: Caldwell County Deed Books 1840-present
source_profiles.coverage_notes: All real estate conveyances, land transactions. Indexes complete from 1840; images from early 1900s forward.

source_profiles.source_name: Caldwell County Probate Records and Will Books 1830-present
source_profiles.repository: Caldwell County Clerk of Superior Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, probate records, guardianships, settlements. Probate books complete from 1830.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some original wills missing; recorded wills intact.

source_profiles.source_name: Caldwell County Marriage Licenses 1841-present
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete from county formation.

source_profiles.source_name: DigitalNC Caldwell County Historic Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: 7 titles. Lenoir News-Topic (1875-1924, 2,734 issues) is primary title.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1868 courthouse fire destroyed court records. Deed books in Register's office and original wills in Clerk's office survived.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Smallest NC county. Formed April 8, 1777 from Pasquotank. Named for Charles Pratt, 1st Earl Camden, who opposed American colonial taxation. Tidewater location (northeastern NC). Colonial Pasquotank records (pre-1777) contain Camden ancestors. For five years (1777-1782) courthouse held in Joseph Jones's residence.
county_metadata.research_tips: Search Pasquotank County records before 1777 for earlier generations. Land conveyances from 1739 survived fire. Use colonial estate files (Secretary of State) for wills prior to 1760. Published history: 'Three Hundred Years Along the Pasquotank' by Pugh (1957). Small county — in-person research productive.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Three Hundred Years Along the Pasquotank (Jesse Forbes Pugh, 1957)
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: limited; land records from 1739 in office; online search from 1999 forward only

source_profiles.source_name: Camden County Deed Books 1739-present
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land conveyances from 1739. Survived 1868 fire. Online search from 1999 forward; earlier records in office.

source_profiles.source_name: Camden County Will Books and Estate Records 1752-1798+
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Oldest will book covers Pasquotank/Camden jointly 1752-1798 (Book K). Pre-1760 wills filed with Secretary of State (colonial estate files).

source_profiles.source_name: NC Colonial Land Grants — Pasquotank/Camden area
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original land grants from Lords Proprietors and Crown. 216,000+ records with direct image links.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Pre-1713 records lost through parent county (Craven) Indian raid fire in 1712.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of oldest NC counties. Formed 1722 from Craven Precinct as precinct of Bath County. Full county status 1739 when Bath County abolished. Coastal county with strong maritime heritage. Named for John Carteret, 2nd Earl Granville. Beaufort was early colonial port. Precinct deeds begin 1713.
county_metadata.research_tips: Search Craven County records 1712-1722 for early Carteret ancestors. Maritime records valuable for coastal ancestry. St. John's Parish (est. 1742) records in 3 volumes are critical for early vital records. Published deed abstracts Books A-F (1713-1759) available. Land grants from 1720s-1730s. Passenger/crew lists for Morehead City 1908-1958 on FamilySearch.
county_metadata.heritage_book: The Heritage of Carteret County, North Carolina (Heritage Books, Inc.)
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: images and indexes; precinct deeds from 1713

source_profiles.source_name: Carteret County Deed Books (including Precinct Deeds) 1713-present
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Precinct deeds 1713-1722 from Craven books; Carteret county deeds 1722+. Published abstracts Books A-F (1713-1759).

source_profiles.source_name: Carteret County Wills and Estate Papers 1663-1978
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estate papers, probate records. Probate from 1744 forward complete.

source_profiles.source_name: St. John's Parish Vestry Books, Beaufort 1742-1843
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Three volumes extant. Vestry records, baptisms, burials, church transactions. Critical for early vital records in colonial period.

source_profiles.source_name: DigitalNC Carteret County Historic Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: 5 titles. Carteret County News-Times (1948-1963), Beaufort News (1915-1944), Carteret County Telephone (1881-1885).

county_metadata.record_losses: Some records said to have been destroyed by militia troops during Reconstruction (1865+). No courthouse fire documented. Impact limited.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Piedmont county on Virginia border. Named for Richard Caswell (1729-1789), first NC governor post-independence. Formed 1777 from Orange County (effective February 1778). Early records may reference Orange County. Reconstruction-era record losses documented but not catastrophic.
county_metadata.research_tips: Check Orange County for pre-1777 ancestry. Tax lists (1777, 1780, 1784) available at NC Digital Collections — valuable for pre-1790 census substitute. Published deed books 1777-1817. Published will books 1777-1843 available on Ancestry. Multiple heritage/history books including Powell's bicentennial history (1977). Gunn Memorial Public Library (Yanceyville) has local history room.
county_metadata.heritage_book: The Heritage of Caswell County, North Carolina (Jeannine D. Whitlow, ed., 1985)
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: images and indexes; Old Index Books by grantor/grantee searchable online

source_profiles.source_name: Caswell County Deed Books 1777-present
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete from county formation. Indexes and images available online.

source_profiles.source_name: Caswell County Will Books and Probate Records 1777-1843+
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published will books cover 1777-1843 plus 1784 tax list and 1794-1819 Guardians' Accounts. Probate from 1771.

source_profiles.source_name: Caswell County Tax Lists 1777-1784
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax lists for 1777, 1780, 1784. Head of household names, acreage, property. Valuable pre-census substitute.

source_profiles.source_name: NC Militia Records — Caswell County Troop Returns 1812-1813
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Troop returns by brigade (6th, 16th). War of 1812 militia records. Male names, unit, rank.

source_profiles.source_name: DigitalNC Caswell County Historic Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Caswell News (1885-1888), Caswell Messenger (1926-1927, 95 issues).
